titleStmt.title: Two Roman Tax Receipts from Uppsala

encodingDesc.ab: Converted from a Word document for Pylon.

docTitle.titlePart: Two Roman Tax Receipts from Uppsala

div.note: This publication brings to fruition work begun during the 2024 Summer Institute of Papyrology in Florence. We remain grateful to the participants, contributors and organizers for their support. The article was made possible thanks to The American Society of Papyrologists and Istituto Papirologico «G. Vitelli». Special thanks are due to Todd Hickey, who encouraged the authors and offered substantive assistance, including his images of the ostraka. This publication appears with the permission of Ludmila Werkström, Curator, Uppsala University Museum.

div.p: The two tax receipts presented below come from the collection of the Victoria Museum of Egyptian Antiquities at the Gustavianum, Uppsala University Museum, and were acquired during the last quarter of the nineteenth century. For more information about the ostraka and their provenance see Hickey 2010: 258 with fn. 2 and the literature cited there.

Sand-colored pottery with a red-orange writing surface. The rotation lines run perpendicular to the writing. Though the sherd is broken on its right side, the left and the majority of the upper writing margins are completely preserved; owing to the diagonal nature of the break, the loss in the upper right is considerably less than that in the lower right.

This is a new text that can be assigned to the Asklas-Ptolemaios-dossier
The attribution of O.Strasb. 1 262 to the Asklas-Ptolemaios-dossier allows for a more precise determination of the ostrakon’s provenance, narrowing the previously general designation of “Upper Egypt” to Thebes. (see the table below), which was first identified by Worp 1990. The dossier brings together tax receipts issued by an Asklas and by Ptolemaios son of Asklas, both attested as collectors of the bath tax. It cannot be established with certainty that the Asklas of these receipts is identical with the father of Ptolemaios, or indeed that all the attestations refer to one and the same man; cf. Worp 1990: 253 f. and Heilporn 2009: 88 n. 117. The receipts are nevertheless assembled here as a single dossier on several grounds: they derive from the same locality (Thebes); they concern not merely the same office but one specific tax, the bath tax; they fall within a coherent span of years (14–35 CE); and, if the Asklas of the receipts is indeed the father named in Ptolemaios’ patronymic, a father and son both collecting that same tax would accord with the pattern by which such fiscal duties were handed on within a family (cf. Worp 1990: 254). Though none of these considerations is decisive on its own, taken together they make it reasonable to treat the material as a working dossier, pending further evidence.

The tax attested in this ostrakon was paid to a telōnēs balaneiou, which identifies it as the bath tax. While Greek sources consistently use the same terminology (balaneutikon) for the two bath taxes that were collected in Roman Thebes, the Demotic texts distinguish between ḥḏ (n) s.t-ỉywn (“bath money”) and ẖn.t n tꜣ s.t-ỉywn (“payment for the bath”)
Particular thanks go to Sandra Lippert for her advice on the Demotic.. This distinction appears to be unique to Thebes. The ḥḏ (n) s.t-ỉywn was a personal tax collected by the bank or praktores argyrikon, together with the laographia and chōmatikon; cf. Heilporn 2009, 89. The ẖn.t n tꜣ s.t-ỉywn, which Heilporn (2009: 88) tentatively identifies with the second balaneutikon, appears to have been collected separately by the balaneutai, praktōres balaneutikou or balanēou or telōnai balaneutikou; see Heilporn 2009: 88. If Heilporn’s identification is correct, the official named in the present ostrakon points to the ẖn.t n tꜣ s.t-ỉywn rather than to the ḥḏ (n) s.t-ỉywn.

VM 2439 records a payment of two drachmae for the ẖn.t n tꜣ s.t-ỉywn. In the first-century receipts the amount is stated more often than in those of the second century, and two drachmae is the usual sum; one drachma occurs less frequently and may represent a partial payment, while four drachmae is exceptional; cf. Heilporn 2009: 88. Payment in kind is also attested; cf. Heilporn 2009: 88 f. The same amount of two drachmae appears in SB 1 4519, likewise dated to December 14 CE and naming Asklas as tax farmer, and in O.Leid. 45.

note.p: 1 The name Asklas is a hypocoristic form of the name Asklepiades; cf. Masson 1990, vol. 1: 299–302 and TM Nam 2362. A tax collector named Asklas is attested on several ostraka from Thebes dated to 14–35 CE (see table below, nos. 4–10, and possibly nos. 1, 2, 11; cf. Worp 1990: 253 f.). VM 2439, in which Asklas is designated as τελών(ης) βαλανείου, adds another instance to this dossier.

An Asklas is also attested as collector of other taxes, possibly as telōnēs gerdiōn and as collector of a further, perhaps religious, tax (cf. O.Bodl. 2 739 (29 CE, Thebes): πράκτωρ θε̣α̣γ̣ῶν̣; not specifiable further). Whether this is the same man as the collector of the bath tax cannot be determined. The titles praktōr and telōnēs are possibly interchangeable, see Heilporn 2009: 88 with fn. 117.

After the eta there are traces of what may be a chi or a kappa. If so, Aρβη.[ could be the beginning of a name like Ἁρβῆχις or Ἁρβήκις, which are Greek forms of the Egyptian theophoric name Harpbekis; cf. NB Dem. vol. I. 11 p. 802 and TM Nam 286.

2 The name Psenchonsis means “The son of Chonsou”; see NB Dem. vol. I. 4 pp. 256 f. and TM Nam 970. Undeclined patronyms are a common feature of the identification of the parties in both tax receipts edited here; see also ll. 2 and 4 of VM 2556 below.

Typically, τὸ βαλανευτικόν follows the verb ἀπέχω; less often the tax is expressed simply as τὸν φόρον, without the name of the tax, as in O.Leid. 45 – an ostrakon belonging to the same dossier and probably issued by the same tax collector (see comm. ad l. 4). On the Uppsala ostrakon the phi visible after the masculine article excludes βαλανείου and βαλανευτικοῦ
The reading βαλανικοῦ is still occasionally encountered. It goes back to Wilcken (1899: 165f.), who had only abbreviated sources at his disposal, and was subsequently adopted by others; it is listed in Preisigke’s Wörterbuch (1931: 231). Since Préaux (1934: 128–132), however, the reading has been corrected on the basis of new finds, and the correction is now generally accepted; cf. BL 9, p. 1. and points to τὸν φ[όρον]. The abbreviation φόρ(ον) in O.Leid. 45 (cf. Wilcken 1912: 213 n. 4) is conditioned by the broken edge and need not be reproduced here. O.Bodl. II 1976.2 (4 BCE, Thebes) may serve as a contemporary parallel for the present reconstruction, since there too the article τοῦ for the tax year follows directly upon “τ(ὸν) φό(ρον)”.

3 It appears that not many letters followed Aρβη.[ in l. 1, given that l. 2 begins with the second part of the name, Psenchonsis. The imperial titulature Καίσαρος Σεβαστοῦ is typically given in Asklas’ tax receipts; among those with a Greek dating formula, in the Asklas-Ptolemaios dossier Sebastos is omitted only in O.Wilck. 2 390 (39/40 or 43/44, Thebes) and O.Wilck. 2 1037 (39/40 or 43/44, Thebes) (both receipts were issued by Ptolemaios, son of Asklas); cf. also Bureth 1964: 25–28, where the Καίσαρος Σεβαστοῦ-titulature is shown to be used much more frequently than the abbreviated version Καίσαρος. In the present ostrakon, however, the available space suggests that the shorter titulature Καίσαρος alone was used. Verschleifung seems unlikely given the hand. Thus, if one takes the shorter imperial titulature as a reference point, there are possibly six letters missing in this line, which would then consist of 22 letters in total. Nevertheless, the titulature Καίσαρος Σεβαστοῦ cannot be excluded, as it remains unknown whether the ostrakon originally extended further to the right in the lower lines.

4 There is a hook-alpha signaling the abbreviation ἔγρα(ψα). As for the name of the scribe of the ostrakon, it may have been Πετεμενῶφις Πελαίου, as Asklas seems to have worked with him at this time and the handwriting is not dissimilar; cf. O.Petr. Mus. 272 (14 CE, Thebes) and O.Leid. 45 with the editors’ comm. ad l. 4.
A special thanks is extended to the Petrie Museum of Egyptian and Sudanese Archaeology, as well as the Rijksmuseum van Oudheden, for providing us with high-resolution photographs of the aforementioned ostraka. The name Πετεμενῶφις could be abbreviated as Πετεμενῶφ(ις) (O.Petr. Mus. 272) or Πετεμενῶ(φις) (O.Leid. 45), giving 26 or 25 letters in total in l. 4.

div.head:
Table: Texts of the Asklas-Ptolemaios-dossier (adapted from Worp 1990, 254)
Worp suggests that there is also a second son of Asklas, Pasemis. This observation is based on O.Leid. 46 = O.Wilck. 1321 (22–23 CE, Thebes) where one Pasemis, son of Asklas is mentioned, and on O.Wilck. 1263 (19–20 CE, Thebes) (=TM 77647), where the same name appears without a patronymic, cf. Worp 1990, 254.

row.cell: A Tiberian dating seems more likely; cf. BL 2.1.21
Demotic; see O.Louvre p. 232–233, no. 648: G. Mattha suggests the bath tax in l. 2
two more drachmae were also collected
the person seems to be in arrears, as the tax is described as πρόλοιπον
the person seems to be in arrears, as the tax is described as πρόλοιπον
the person seems to be in arrears, as the tax is described as πρόλοιπον
Worp (1990, 254) prefers the dating 41/42, further alternatives see BL Demotic 2005B, p. 450; Demotic
Is this okay?the person seems to be in arrears, as the tax is described as πρόλοιπον;we thank Nikolaos Gonis for confirming Tait’s reading regarding the taxpayer’s name
the person seems to be in arrears, as the tax is described as πρόλοιπον
the person seems to be in arrears, as the tax is described as πρόλοιπον

div.p: Right bottom corner broken with the endings of the last two lines lost. Otherwise, all margins are preserved. The text consists of 6 lines of around 20 letters each. The sherd has a red-orange writing surface.

Two other attestations of tax collector, Hones, son of Petemenophis, come from Thebes (for details see commentary to line 1). Most of the ostraka from the Uppsala collection are of Theban provenance (O.Ups. 3–16). The recipient’s name, Pamonthes, is characteristic of the Theban region (see TM Nam 720). Therefore it’s clear that the ostrakon comes from Thebes.

This receipt acknowledges the payment of a monthly tax without denomination in the amount of two drachmae. For examples of this, see O.Bodl. 2 1048–1062 (especially No. 1048, 1051, 1052), O.Berl. 32 (29 Aug.–26. Dec. 108 CE, Elephantine). The closest parallels are O.Berl. 25 (30. Aug.–28. Oct. 55 CE, Thebes) and O.Berl. 27 (30 Aug.–28 Oct. 63 CE, Thebes).

Taking into account the use of the word τέλος in other ostraka and the fact that the tax is paid monthly, the editors’ suggestion that O.Bodl. 2 1048–1062 are “supposed to be taxes on trades”
O. Bodl. 2 p. 136. is followed also in this edition.

For the undetermined taxes understood as taxes on trades see most recently the commentary on O.Petr. Mus. 277 (45 CE, Thebes) and O.Petr. Mus. 278 (1 March 112 CE, Thebes), as well as Wallace 1938: 192–213.

The fact that the tax collector was a bath tax collector around the same time unfortunately does not help, as the monthly amount of two drachmae do not correspond to the typical amounts recorded for bath taxes.

note.p:
1 The same tax farmer and his associates can be found in: O.Strasb. 1 180 (37 CE, Thebes) and probably also in O.Bodl. 2 661 (40 CE, Thebes). The name Ὁνῆς is a variant (TM NamVar 35465) of Egyptian Αὑνῆς (TM Nam 354), Demotic Ḥwn
NB Dem., vol. I.2 p. 778., meaning “the young one”, with possible theophoric origin. It is attested across Egypt. The name Πετεμενῶφις (TM Nam 831) means “the one whom Amon of Luxor has given”
NB Dem., vol. I.1 p. 282. and is typical for Theban region.

2 A similar mistake may be found in O.Cair. 60.2 (170 CE, Hermonthites): μέτοχω and O.Bodl. 2 655.1 (21 CE, Thebes): μετωχω instead of μέτοχοι. Usually, in tax receipts, both terms, μέτοχοι and τελῶναι, would be abbreviated. The fact that we find it in unabbreviated form is somewhat surprising, as it resulted in insufficient space for the full name of the recipient. Only Παμω is written inline, with ν elevated and θ superscript. Nevertheless, in the next line the scribe used two abbreviations.

The theophoric name Pamonthes (TM Nam 720) is typical for Theban region as it means “the one of Montu”
NB Dem., vol. I.1 p. 372–373., Montu being the falcon headed war god of Thebes (TM God 51).

3 The reading at the beginning of the line is not easy, even though all the strokes are perfectly visible. The ligature of αρ, in such a peculiar shape is uncommon. However, the same ductus appears for example in O.Ber. 1 17.2 in the word ἀριθ̣μῶι (33–70 CE?, Berenike).

The Trismegistos Project attributes the name of Pamonthes’s father to an Egyptian name with Latin roots (TM NamVar 31482 – Varius). However, the presence of the name Ouaros in P. Lond. 7 2136.1 (263–229 BCE, Zenon Archive) casts some shadow on such an interpretation. Bearing of a Greek variant of Latin name by an Egyptian so early is unlikely (however not impossible). Therefore, we can also be dealing with a hellenized Egyptian or Semitic name, although there is no corresponding name attested. According to Tavernier 2020: 338 fn. 6 Ouaris is a Greek equivalent of the Lycian personal name Uwihairi. For discussion of this name in relation to Latin Varus and Varius, see Zgusta 1964: 393 §1143-3. Taking into consideration that in the 3rd century BCE Lycia was under Ptolemaic rule, the hypothesis of the Lycian origin of the name mentioned in the ostrakon cannot be fully excluded.

It may be that name Ouaros and Ouaris are two etymologically unrelated names. This interpretation is supported by inscription SB 1 3493.1 (244 CE, Thebes), where Οὐάρις is clearly personal name in the nominative and not a patronymic. The same can be found in O.Bodl. 2 1937.8. In P.Mich. 5 238 R.187 (after 46 CE, Tebtynis) it appears in the accusative, Οὐᾶρον; therefore it can be both Ouaros and Ouaris. Another attestation is O.Ont.Mus. 1 10.3 where the name appears as a patronym, however in the abbreviated form Οὐᾶρ – and thus not helpful in determining the nature of the name.
4 Theogeneios corresponds to Tybi (Hanson 1984: 1112 fn. 15); therefore, Theogeneios in the 5th year of Caligula = 27 December 40 CE / 25 January 41 CE.

Since the classic article of Scott 1931 four more attestations have been published, with this ostrakon being the sixth one (and first attestation on an ostrakon). Other attestations of this month are: P.Oxy. 55 3780.5 (calendar, 40–42 CE, Oxyrhynchos), PSI Com. 9 7.2 (heading of the document, 7 Jan. 41 CE, Tebtynis), BGU 3 713.3 (loan contract, 1 Jan. 42 CE, Soknopaiou Nesos); SB 20 14576.7 (banking list, 14 Jan. 43 CE, Philadelphia) and P.Oxy. 88 5660.2 (receipt for tax on stonecutters, 22 Jan. 42). For the slightly outdated yet very useful discussion of Caligulan months, see Scott 1931: 245–263. Some updates, although focused on the usage in the village of Philadelphia, can be found in Hanson 1984. The most recent work on the honorific months is Dörner 2014: 277–298, and especially about Caligulan months 286–297.

5 The year-sign encircles the epsilon while simultaneously transitioning into the vertical hasta of the gamma. The scribe most likely wrote the epsilon first and, having initially omitted the year-sign, was forced to add it in the margin. He integrated it quite elegantly by merging the sign with the initial gamma of Γαίου.
The word division Αὐτοκρ̣άτορ|ος between lines 5 and 6 is non-standard; the correct syllabification in this case would be Αὐτοκρ̣άτο|ρος.
6 Given the space left at the end of the last line, which is ca. 8 letters, the following conjectures are possible:

listBibl.bibl: Bureth, P. (1964) Les titulatures impériales dans les papyrus, les ostraca et les inscriptions d’Égypte (30 a.C.–284 p.C.). Brussels.
Dörner, N. (2014) Feste und Opfer für den Gott Caesar: Kommunikationsprozesse im Rahmen des Kaiserkultes im römischen Ägypten der julisch-claudischen Zeit (30 v. Chr.–68 n. Chr.). Rahden/Westph.

Hanson A. E. (1984) “Caligulan Month-Names at Philadelphia and Related Matters.” in: Atti del XVII Congresso Internazionale di Papirologia, Napoli, 19–26 maggio 1983, vol. 3: Papirologia documentaria, papirologia araba. Naples, 1107–1118.
Heilporn, P. (ed.) (2009), Thèbes et ses taxes. Recherches sur la fiscalité en Égypte romaine (Ostraca de Strasbourg II) (EAHA). Paris.
Hickey, T. (2010) “Ostraca Upsaliensia (Part I),” APF 56: 258–273.
Johnson, A. Ch. (1936) An Economic Survey of Ancient Rome, vol. 2: Roman Egypt to the Reign of Diocletian. Baltimore.
Lüddeckens, E. (1984) Demotisches Namenbuch, vol. 1.4. Wiesbaden. (=NB Dem.)
Lüddeckens, E. (1992) Demotisches Namenbuch, vol. 1.11. Wiesbaden.
Masson, O. (1990) Onomastica Graeca selecta, vol. 1: Introduction et index, ed. by C. Tobias and L. Dubois. Paris.
Préaux, C. (1934) “La taxe des bains dans l’Égypte romaine. A propos de l’ostracon de Bruxelles,” CE 9: 129–132.

Preisigke, F. (1931) Wörterbuch der griechischen Papyrusurkunden mit Einschluß der griechischen Inschriften und Aufschriften, Ostraka, Mumienschilder usw. aus Ägypten, ed. by E. Kießling, vol. 3: Besondere Wörterliste. Berlin.
Scott, K. (1931) “Greek and Roman honorific months,” YCS 2: 199–278.
Tavernier, J. (2020) “Lycian Erimñuha” in: Hrozný and Hittite: the first hundred years: proceedings of the International Conference held at Charles University, Prague, 11-14 November 2015, 382–395.
Wallace, S. L. (1938) Taxation in Egypt from Augustus to Diocletian. Princeton.
Wilcken, U. (1899) Griechische Ostraka aus Ägypten und Nubien. Ein Beitrag zur antiken Wirtschaftsgeschichte, vol. 1. Leipzig – Berlin.
Wilcken, U. (1912) Grundzüge und Chrestomathie der Papyruskunde, vol. 1: Historischer Teil. 1. Hälfte: Grundzüge. Leipzig.
Worp, K. A. (1990) “Remarks on Some Greek Marginalia in Demotic Tax Receipts,” ZPE 80: 253–256.
Zgusta, L. (1964) Kleinasiatische Personennamen, Prague.
Bureth, P. (1964) Les titulatures impériales dans les papyrus, les ostraca et les inscriptions d’Égypte (30 a.C.–284 p.C.). Brussels.
Dörner, N. (2014) Feste und Opfer für den Gott Caesar: Kommunikationsprozesse im Rahmen des Kaiserkultes im römischen Ägypten der julisch-claudischen Zeit (30 v. Chr.–68 n. Chr.). Rahden/Westph.
Gignac F. T. (1981) A Grammar of the Greek Papyri of the Roman and Byzantine Periods (Testi e Documenti per lo Studio dell’Antichità 55), vol. 2: Morphology. Milan.

Hanson A. E. (1984) “Caligulan Month-Names at Philadelphia and Related Matters.” in: Atti del XVII Congresso Internazionale di Papirologia, Napoli, 19–26 maggio 1983, vol. 3: Papirologia documentaria, papirologia araba. Naples, 1107–1118.
Heilporn, P. (ed.) (2009), Thèbes et ses taxes. Recherches sur la fiscalité en Égypte romaine (Ostraca de Strasbourg II) (EAHA). Paris.
Hickey, T. (2010) “Ostraca Upsaliensia (Part I),” APF 56: 258–273.
Johnson, A. Ch. (1936) An Economic Survey of Ancient Rome, vol. 2: Roman Egypt to the Reign of Diocletian. Baltimore.
Lüddeckens, E. (1984) Demotisches Namenbuch, vol. 1.4. Wiesbaden. (=NB Dem.)

Preisigke, F. (1931) Wörterbuch der griechischen Papyrusurkunden mit Einschluß der griechischen Inschriften und Aufschriften, Ostraka, Mumienschilder usw. aus Ägypten, ed. by E. Kießling, vol. 3: Besondere Wörterliste. Berlin.

div.ab:
Ἀσκλᾶς τελώνης βαλανείουβαλανήου Αρβη
ΨενχώνσιοςΨενχῶνσις χαίρειν. ἀπέχω τὸν φόρον
τοῦ α ἔτους δραχμὰς β. ἔτους α Τιβερίου Καίσαρος
Χοίακ η. ἔγραψα Πετ

availability.p: © Heidelberger Gesamtverzeichnis der griechischen Papyrusurkunden Ägyptens. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 License.

sourceDesc.p: The contents of this document are generated from SOSOL.

div.p: Asklas, farmer of the bath tax, to Arbe[…], son of Psenchonsis, greetings. I have received the tax for the 1st year, 2 drachmae, year 1 of Tiberius Ca[esar], Choiak 8. I, Pet[…], wrote it.

titleStmt.title: Receipt for an undetermined monthly tax

encodingDesc.p: This file encoded to comply with EpiDoc Guidelines and Schema version 8

titleStmt.title: Receipt for an undetermined monthly tax

availability.p: © Duke Databank of Documentary Papyri. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 License.

body.head: Receipt for an undetermined monthly tax

div.ab:
Ὁνῆς ΠετεμενώφιοςΠετεμενῶφις καὶ
μέτοχοιμετόχων τελῶναι Παμώνθῃ
Οὐᾶρις χαίρειν. ἔχομεν τὸ τέλος τοῦ
μηνὸς ΘεογενείουΘεογένιος δραχμὰς β.
ἔτους ε Γαίου Καίσαρος Αὐτοκράτορ
ος Σεβαστοῦ μηνὸς

titleStmt.title: Receipt for an undetermined monthly tax

availability.p: © Heidelberger Gesamtverzeichnis der griechischen Papyrusurkunden Ägyptens. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 License.

sourceDesc.p: The contents of this document are generated from SOSOL.

div.p: Ηοnes, son of Petemenophis, and partners, tax farmers, to Pamonthes, son of Varius, greetings. We have received the tax of 2 drachmae for the month of Theogeneios. Year 5th of Gaius Caesar Imperator Augustus, month (month name, day date).
